raw_response: {"rating": -1.2, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report in the form of a fact-check. The author's own prose consistently frames the White House's data choice as inferior while elevating alternative measures that produce smaller figures favorable to undermining Trump's claim. The cumulative effect of these framing choices produces a modest Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the White House's data selection as 'cherry-picked,' a pejorative editorial term implying deliberate manipulation, rather than a neutral description such as 'selected' or 'chose'; (2) The structure of the piece systematically layers downward revisions — from $546 to $121 to $26 — each step further undercutting Trump's claim, with no equivalent acknowledgment that the White House figure is drawn from an official BLS dataset; (3) The author notes that Biden's equivalent period showed a $884 wage gain under the preferred metric, which is presented as undercutting Trump's narrative — this comparative framing introduces a favorable reference point for the prior Democratic administration that goes beyond the narrow fact-check scope; (4) The sourcing balance nominally includes a center-right economist (Holtz-Eakin) and a liberal economist (Baker), but both are quoted agreeing against the White House's preferred measure, producing a one-sided evidentiary conclusion despite the appearance of ideological balance; (5) The inflation-adjusted figure of $26 is foregrounded in the ruling section with emphasis, even though this metric uses the smaller, less-preferred household survey the article itself identified as 'highly erratic,' an inconsistency the author does not address in their own prose."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report in the form of a fact-check. The author's own prose consistently frames the White House's data choice as inferior while elevating alternative measures that produce smaller figures favorable to undermining Trump's claim. The cumulative effect of these framing choices produces a modest Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the White House's data selection as 'cherry-picked,' a pejorative editorial term implying deliberate manipulation, rather than a neutral description such as 'selected' or 'chose'; (2) The structure of the piece systematically layers downward revisions — from $546 to $121 to $26 — each step further undercutting Trump's claim, with no equivalent acknowledgment that the White House figure is drawn from an official BLS dataset; (3) The author notes that Biden's equivalent period showed a $884 wage gain under the preferred metric, which is presented as undercutting Trump's narrative — this comparative framing introduces a favorable reference point for the prior Democratic administration that goes beyond the narrow fact-check scope; (4) The sourcing balance nominally includes a center-right economist (Holtz-Eakin) and a liberal economist (Baker), but both are quoted agreeing against the White House's preferred measure, producing a one-sided evidentiary conclusion despite the appearance of ideological balance; (5) The inflation-adjusted figure of $26 is foregrounded in the ruling section with emphasis, even though this metric uses the smaller, less-preferred household survey the article itself identified as 'highly erratic,' an inconsistency the author does not address in their own prose.

explanation: This is a news report in the form of a fact-check. The author's own prose consistently frames the White House's data choice as inferior while elevating alternative measures that produce smaller figures favorable to undermining Trump's claim. The cumulative effect of these framing choices produces a modest Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the White House's data selection as 'cherry-picked,' a pejorative editorial term implying deliberate manipulation, rather than a neutral description such as 'selected' or 'chose'; (2) The structure of the piece systematically layers downward revisions — from $546 to $121 to $26 — each step further undercutting Trump's claim, with no equivalent acknowledgment that the White House figure is drawn from an official BLS dataset; (3) The author notes that Biden's equivalent period showed a $884 wage gain under the preferred metric, which is presented as undercutting Trump's narrative — this comparative framing introduces a favorable reference point for the prior Democratic administration that goes beyond the narrow fact-check scope; (4) The sourcing balance nominally includes a center-right economist (Holtz-Eakin) and a liberal economist (Baker), but both are quoted agreeing against the White House's preferred measure, producing a one-sided evidentiary conclusion despite the appearance of ideological balance; (5) The inflation-adjusted figure of $26 is foregrounded in the ruling section with emphasis, even though this metric uses the smaller, less-preferred household survey the article itself identified as 'highly erratic,' an inconsistency the author does not address in their own prose.
